title: Dyeing with indigo on cotton
subtitle: Building blue on cotton fabric using a simple sodium hydrosulphite and soda ash reduction vat
excerpt: Indigo does not dissolve in water directly. To dye with it, the pigment must be chemically reduced to its soluble leuco form in an alkaline reducing vat. The fibre is dipped, then lifted into the air where the indigo oxidises back to insoluble blue and fixes to the fibre. Multiple dips build depth of colour. Cotton requires a good scour before vat dyeing for even uptake.
sourceNotes: Sodium hydrosulphite reduction vat for indigo is standard contemporary natural dyeing practice. Cotton preparation and multiple-dip colour building are documented in dye guild workshops and contemporary instruction.

attrs.body: Sodium hydrosulphite is irritating to skin and airways. Wear gloves and work in ventilated conditions.
attrs.heading: Safety

content.text: turns fibre from yellow-green to blue on contact with air. Each dip adds a layer of colour: a single dip produces a pale sky blue, five or six dips produce a medium navy.
between dips is as important as the time in the vat.

items.name: Stainless steel or plastic vat (5-10 litre capacity)

content.text: Mix the indigo powder to a paste with a small amount of cold water. In the vat, dissolve the soda ash in warm water (about 50 degrees Celsius) and add the indigo paste. Stir gently in one direction to avoid introducing air. Sprinkle the sodium hydrosulphite over the surface and stir gently. Leave undisturbed for 15-20 minutes. The vat is ready when the surface has a copper-bronze metallic sheen and the liquid below is yellow-green.

Cotton must be thoroughly scoured to remove sizing, oils, and processing residues that block dye uptake. Simmer the cotton in a solution of washing soda (2-3 g per litre) for 30 minutes. Rinse well and keep wet until dyeing. Do not allow the cotton to dry between scouring and dyeing.

Squeeze excess water from the cotton. Submerge it slowly into the vat, keeping it below the surface for 3-5 minutes. Move gently to ensure even contact, but avoid splashing or introducing air bubbles. Lift the cotton slowly from the vat and squeeze it gently back into the vat. Hold the cotton in the air for 10-15 minutes: you will see it change from yellow-green to blue as it oxidises. Rinse in cold water, then repeat for further dips.

After the final dip and oxidation, rinse the cotton thoroughly in cold water. Add a small amount of white vinegar to the final rinse to neutralise the alkalinity from the soda ash. This helps preserve the cotton fibre. Dispose of the spent vat by neutralising with vinegar, then diluting heavily with water before disposal.

items.fix: The vat needs more reducing agent and more time. Add a small amount of sodium hydrosulphite, stir gently in one direction, and leave undisturbed for 15 minutes before testing again.
items.cause: The vat has not reduced: the indigo is still in its insoluble blue form
items.problem: The vat looks blue rather than yellow-green, and the fibre does not pick up colour

items.fix: Squeeze out all air before entering the vat. Open fabric fully and move it slowly underwater to ensure the vat liquid penetrates every part.
items.cause: Air pockets trapped in the fabric during dipping, or fabric folded on itself
items.problem: The blue is uneven with white streaks

items.fix: Allow a full 15 minutes of air exposure between each dip. Indigo needs adequate oxygen contact to complete the colour shift. Rushing the oxidation step limits colour depth.
items.problem: The colour is greener than blue and does not shift after oxidation
